coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric and photothermal dual-mode biosensing platform based on nanozyme-functionalized flower-like DNA structures for tumor-derived exosome detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoyue
Zhu, Xueying
Li, Yuanfang
Hai, Xin
Bi, Sai
coredata.dc:description: Tumor-derived exosomes can be served as a kind of promising biomarkers for early diagnosis of cancers. Herein, a colorimetric/photothermal dual-mode exosomes sensing platform is developed for human breast cancer cell (MCF-7)-derived exosomes based on encapsulation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine-loaded graphene quantum dot nanozymes (TMB-GQDzymes) into DNA flowers (DFs) via rolling circle amplification (RCA). To achieve specific detection, EpCAM aptamer for MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes is immobilized on the well plate, while the complementary sequence of another CD63 aptamer is designed into the circular template to obtain abundant capture probes. Benefitting from the dual-aptamer recognition strategy, a sandwich structure of EpCAM aptamer/exosomes/TMB-GQDzymes@DFs is formed, in which the GQDzymes can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. The resulting products of TMB oxidation (oxTMB) can induce not only the absorption changes but also a near-infrared (NIR) laser-driven photothermal effect, achieving dual-mode detection of exosomes with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1027 particles/μL (colorimetry) and 2170 particles/μL (photothermal detection), respectively. In addition, this sensing platform has demonstrated excellent performance to well distinguish breast cancer patients from healthy individuals in serum samples analysis. Overall, the proposed dual-readout biosensor opens promising prospects for exosome detection in biological study and clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
DNA flower
Rolling circle amplification
Exosome
Dual-mode biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of catalytic activity of GQDzymes
Exosomes extraction from cell culture supernatant
Immobilization of EpCAM aptamer on 96-well plate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Youth Innovation Technology Program of Shandong Province
Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province

Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22004077 and 22076087 ), the Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province ( ZR2020JQ08 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M671988 ), the Youth Innovation Technology Program of Shandong Province ( 2019KJC029 ), and the Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science , MOE ( M2022-6 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of TMB-GQDzymes synthesis. (B) TEM images of GQDzymes and TMB-GQDzymes. Scale bar: 100 nm. (C) AFM images and profile height analysis along the white lines of GQDzymes and TMB-GQDzymes. Scale bar: 500 nm. (D) Kinetic curve of UV–Vis absorbance at 650 nm for TMB, GQDzymes and TMB-GQDzymes in the presence of H2O2, respectively. (E) UV–Vis adsorption spectra of TMB-GQDzymes with different concentrations of TMB (0–0.24 mg/mL) in the presence of H2O2, respectively. (F) FTIR spectra of TMB, GQDzymes and TMB-GQDzymes, respectively.

(A) Schematic diagram of circularization of template DNA and encapsulation of TMB-GQDzymes in DFs via RCA-based self-assembly. (B) Native PAGE characterization. Lane 1: template, lane 2: primer, lane 3: annealed template/primer hybrid, lane 4: template/primer hybrid treated with T4 DNA ligase, lane 5: TMB-GQDzymes@DFs. (C) SEM images of DFs and TMB-GQDzymes@DFs. Scale bar: 1 μm. (D) TEM images of DFs and TMB-GQDzymes@DFs. Scale bar: 500 nm. (E) Zeta potential analysis of TMB, GQDzymes, TMB-GQDzymes, DFs and TMB-GQDzymes@DFs. (F) HAADF-STEM images and EDS elemental mapping of DFs and TMB-GQDzymes@DFs. Scale bar: 500 nm. (G) Organic elemental analysis results of DFs and TMB-GQDzymes@DFs.

(A) Schematic illustration of catalytic reaction based on TMB-GQDzymes@DFs. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the proposed biosensor in response to different concentrations of MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes. (C) Linear relationship between ΔA and CExosome. (D) Photothermal imaging of the proposed biosensor in response to different concentrations of MCF-7 cells-derived exosomes under 808 nm laser irradiation for 30 s. (E) Linear relationship between ΔT and CExosome. Note: a-i represents the concentration of exosomes: 0, 2 × 103, 8 × 103, 1.5 × 104, 2.0 × 104, 2.5 × 104, 3.0 × 104, 3.5 × 104, and 4.0 × 104 particles/μL.

Specificity of the dual-mode biosensor towards MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes compared to a series of interfering substances, including BSA (100 μg/mL), CEA (100 μg/mL), GSH (10 mM), glucose (10 mM), Na+ (10 mM) and K+ (10 mM) using (A) colorimetric mode and (B) photothermal mode, respectively. Specificity of the dual-mode biosensor for the detection of exosomes derived from L02 and MCF-7 cells using (C) colorimetric mode and (D) photothermal mode, respectively.

Detection of serum samples from healthy volunteers (n = 10) and breast cancer patients (n = 10) using (A) colorimetric mode and (B) photothermal mode, respectively. (C and D) Boxplots corresponding to the data of A and B, respectively (***P < 0.001).

Schematic illustration of colorimetric/photothermal dual-mode biosensor for the detection of MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes based on dual-aptamer recognition using DNA flowers-encapsulated nanozymes as probes.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric and photothermal dual-mode biosensing platform based on nanozyme-functionalized flower-like DNA structures for tumor-derived exosome detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tumor-derived exosomes can be served as a kind of promising biomarkers for early diagnosis of cancers. Herein, a colorimetric/photothermal dual-mode exosomes sensing platform is developed for human breast cancer cell (MCF-7)-derived exosomes based on encapsulation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine-loaded graphene quantum dot nanozymes (TMB-GQDzymes) into DNA flowers (DFs) via rolling circle amplification (RCA). To achieve specific detection, EpCAM aptamer for MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes is immobilized on the well plate, while the complementary sequence of another CD63 aptamer is designed into the circular template to obtain abundant capture probes. Benefitting from the dual-aptamer recognition strategy, a sandwich structure of EpCAM aptamer/exosomes/TMB-GQDzymes@DFs is formed, in which the GQDzymes can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. The resulting products of TMB oxidation (oxTMB) can induce not only the absorption changes but also a near-infrared (NIR) laser-driven photothermal effect, achieving dual-mode detection of exosomes with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1027 particles/μL (colorimetry) and 2170 particles/μL (photothermal detection), respectively. In addition, this sensing platform has demonstrated excellent performance to well distinguish breast cancer patients from healthy individuals in serum samples analysis. Overall, the proposed dual-readout biosensor opens promising prospects for exosome detection in biological study and clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Exosomes are nanoscaled extracellular vesicles (30-100 nm in diameter) with a lipid bilayer membrane structure, which are secreted by a variety of cells and widely distributed in biological fluids, such as blood, saliva and cerebrospinal fluid [1–3]. As important intercellular carriers, exosomes carry a wealth of molecular information (e.g. proteins, lipids, RNAs and DNAs) from their parental cells [4]. Moreover, the proteins and nucleic acids carried by cancer cells-derived exosomes are related to the proliferation and metastasis of tumors [5–8]. Hence, the development of sensitive and accurate approaches for the detection of cancer-associated exosomes is of great significance in tumor diagnosis and symptomatic treatment [9]. Up to now, various methods have been exploited for exosomes detection, including nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA) [10–12], Western blot (WB) [13], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [14], and so on. However, these methods are often suffered from the disadvantage of time-consuming and involved large quantities of samples as well as cumbersome preparation process. Furthermore, the relatively low content of tumor-associated exosomes in biological samples with complex composition impedes the exosome-based cancer diagnosis in clinical applications. Thus, it is an urgent need to develop rapid and simple ways for exosomes detection with high accuracy, sensitivity and specificity.

Aptamers are single-stranded oligonucleotides that are selected using Systematic Evolution of Ligands by EXponential enrichment (SELEX), which have demonstrated the merits of high specificity and affinity to targets, good stability, as well as easy synthesis and chemical modification [15]. Up to now, a series of aptamers that can specifically recognize the proteins overexpressed on the surface of cancer cells-derived exosomes, including clusters of differentiation 63 (CD63), epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), and epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM), have been utilized to design various probes for the detection of exosome biomarkers based on DNA nanotechnology [16–21]. Rolling circle amplification (RCA) has proved to be a powerful tool for the construction of DNA nanomaterials. During RCA, the ultralong single-stranded DNAs (ssDNAs) can be produced using a circular DNA template and a primer in the presence of polymerase (e.g., phi 29 DNA polymerase), which can be further self-assembled into the organized nanostructures, such as DNA hydrogels, DNA origami and DNA flowers (DFs) [22–25]. By virtue of sequence programmability, various functional nucleic acids (e.g. aptamers, DNAzymes, antisense DNAs, etc.) can be readily incorporated into the DNA structures by rationally designing the circular template [26–28], while the signal tags (e.g. quantum dots and organic dye molecules) can also be encapsulated into the RCA-based DNA nanomaterials, achieving the fabrication of multifunctional probes for versatile biosensing applications with signal amplification [29].

Nanozymes are a class of nanomaterials with enzyme-like catalytic activity. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes possess some unique merits, including high stability, tunable catalytic activity and low cost, which thus have broad application prospects in the field of biosensing [30,31]. As a carbon-based nanomaterial, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoscaled graphene fragments with intact benzene ring structures and abundant functional groups, which have served as the substrate for grafting aromatic molecules through π-π conjugation [32–36]. In particular, GQDs also exhibit the peroxidase-mimicking activity, which can catalyze the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) into hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The generated ·OH can further oxidize the chromogenic substrates (e.g. 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, TMB) to obtain product (e.g. oxTMB) with obvious color changes for visual assays with high sensitivity and simple operation [37,38]. Furthermore, the oxidization products (e.g. oxTMB) also exhibit the near-infrared (NIR) laser-mediated photothermal effect [39], where the signal readout can be readily measured using a thermometer.

In this work, a sensitive and selective approach for colorimetric/photothermal dual-mode detection of MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes is proposed based on the DNA flowers-encapsulated nanozymes as probes through RCA self-assembly (Scheme 1 ). Inspired by the specific recognition of nucleic acid aptamers for protein CD63 overexpressed on MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes and the signal amplification of RCA strategy, the linear amplification of CD63 aptamers is realized by rationally designing their complementary sequences in RCA templates, which are further self-assembled into DNA flowers (DFs) with enhanced binding affinity to exosomes driven by liquid crystallization. In particular, the RCA-based DFs also exhibit the porous structures and large surface area to encapsulate chromogenic substrate TMB-loaded graphene quantum dot nanozymes (TMB-GQDzymes) by a one-pot method. Meanwhile, another EpCAM aptamer is immobilized on the 96-well plate and used as the capture probe to recognize MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes. As a result, a sandwich structure of EpCAM aptamer/exosomes/TMB-GQDzymes@DFs is formed at the plate based on dual-aptamer recognition. In this study, the TMB-GQDzymes are obtained by π-π conjugation and electrostatic adsorption, which have been demonstrated as a novel nanozyme with peroxidase-mimicking activity to promote the catalyzed oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. The oxTMB exhibits the distinct blue color along with the enhanced characteristic absorption at 650 nm and shows the NIR laser-driven photothermal effect due to its obvious absorption at 650 nm. Thus, a colorimetric/photothermal dual-mode biosensing platform is fabricated to detect MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes with high sensitivity and selectivity, which manifests promising applications in the cancer diagnosis.

All the oligonucleotides used in this work were synthesized and HPLC-purified by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the corresponding sequences are listed in Table S1. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3) and 30% H2O2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). TMB, 4S Red Plus Nucleic Acid Stain, T4 DNA ligase and phi29 DNA polymerase were ordered from BBI Life Sciences (Shanghai, China). Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), N, N, N′, N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) and deoxynucleotides (dNTPs) were obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hieff® Quick exosome isolation kit was purchased from Yeasen Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle medium (DMEM) was purchased from Hyclone (UT, USA). All the reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used directly without further purification. The deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was used in all experiments.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded on a JSM-6390LV field emission electron microscope operating at 10 kV (JEOL, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) images were obtained on a Sigma 500 field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (ZEISS, German). The UV-vis absorption spectrums were determined on a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a slit width of 1 nm. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging was performed using the tapping mode on a CSPM 5500 scanning probe microscope (Being, China). The absorbance at 650 nm was recorded using a Synergy HTX Microplate Reader (BioTek, USA).

First, 0.02 g graphene oxide (GO) was dispersed by ultrasound in 2 mL of pure water, which was transferred to a 100 mL round-bottom flask with the addition of 5 mL concentrated HNO3 and 5 mL concentrated H2SO4. After heated to reflux at 140 °C for 10 h and cooled to room temperature, the excess acid was removed by rotary evaporation. The pH of the obtained raw product was adjusted to 7.0 by adding NaOH and dialyzed over deionized water in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 3000 Da) for 3 days to remove the residual salts. Finally, the powder of GQDzymes was quantified by lyophilization, re-dispersed in PBS buffer (pH 7.0) at 10 mg/mL, and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Different volumes (0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μL) of TMB solution (2.4 mg/mL) was mixed with 450 μL of GQDzymes (10 mg/mL) with a final volume of 500 μL, respectively. The final concentrations of TMB were 0, 0.048, 0.096, 0.144, 0.192 and 0.24 mg/mL, respectively. After reacted at 20 °C for 4 h, the samples were purified using an ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (MWCO: 3000 Da).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of catalytic activity of GQDzymes
ce:section.ce:para: The chromogenic reaction of TMB was used to investigate the catalytic activity of GQDzymes. The 1 μL of TMB solution (2.4 mg/mL), 45 μL of GQDzymes (10 mg/mL) and 50 μL of TMB-GQDzymes (TMB concentration: 0.048 mg/mL) were added into 5 μL of H2O2 (5 mM) in per well of a 96-well plate, respectively. Each mixture was supplemented with deionized water up to a volume of 100 μL. The time-dependent change in absorbance at 650 nm in 2 h of each sample was immediately monitored using a microplate reader.

Firstly, 5 μL of 5′-phosphorylated linear template (100 μM) and 10 μL of primer (100 μM) were mixed in 1 × T4 DNA ligase buffer (40 mM Tris-HCl, 10 mM MgCl2, 10 mM DTT, 0.5 mM ATP, pH 7.8). The solution was heated at 95 °C for 5 min, followed by cooling slowly to 25 °C at a rate of 0.1 °C/s for 3 h to realize the hybridization of template and primer. Subsequently, 5 μL of T4 DNA ligase (5 U/μL) was added. After incubation at 16 °C for 12 h, the T4 ligase was inactivated by heating at 65 °C for 10 min to obtain the circular template for RCA reaction. Then, 2 μL of the as-prepared circular template (22.8 μM), 5 μL of phi29 DNA polymerase (20 U/μL), 10 μL of dNTPs (10 mM) and 10 μL of TMB-GQDzymes were added into 1 × phi29 DNA polymerase buffer. The solution was incubated at 30 °C for 30 h. After that, the solution was heated at 65 °C for 10 min to inactivate the phi29 DNA polymerase and then stabilized at 25 °C for 24 h to separate the DFs. The product was centrifuged at 5000 g for 10 min and washed with ultrapure water for three times to remove the unencapsulated TMB-GQDzymes. The obtained TMB-GQDzymes@DFs were stored at 4 °C for future use.

A 10 μL of each sample was mixed with 2 μL of 6 × loading buffer, and the mixture was analyzed by electrophoresis on a 8% native polyacrylamide gel. The PAGE was run at the voltage of 170 V for 5 min and 110 V for 35 min in 1 × TAE buffer (40 mM Tris, 20 mM acetic acid and 2 mM EDTA, pH 8.0). Then the gel was stained with 4S Red Plus Nucleic Acid Stain for 15 min and imaged using Tanon 2500 R gel imaging system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exosomes extraction from cell culture supernatant
ce:section.ce:para: The human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) and human normal liver cells (L02) were cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin, followed by incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2. After incubation until the cell reached 80%-90% confluence, the cell culture was collected into a 15 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 3000 g for 10 min. Then, the supernatant was collected carefully and transferred to a new centrifuge tube, which was placed on ice for later use. The exosomes were collected from the MCF-7 and L02 cell culture supernatant using the Hieff® Quick exosome isolation kit according to the manufacturer's instructions. A 10 mL of cell culture supernatant and 2.5 mL of exosome isolation solution were vortexed and mixed for 1 min, and then placed at 2-8 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the centrifuge tube containing the mixed solution was taken out and centrifuged at 10,000 g for 60 min at 4 °C. After removing the supernatant, the sediment was collected and resuspended in 100 μL of 1 × PBS, followed by transferred to a new 1.5 mL centrifuge tube. After centrifuged at 12,000 g for 2 min at 4 °C, the resulting supernatant was transferred to an exosome purification column and centrifuged at 3000 g for 10 min at 4 °C. The liquid at the bottom of the tube was the purified exosome particles. The collected exosomes were stored at −80 °C for further use. The size and concentration of exosomes were determined by nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA) (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of EpCAM aptamer on 96-well plate
ce:section.ce:para: A 200 μL of glutaraldehyde (5 mM) was firstly added to a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Then the plate was washed three times with PBS buffer (pH 5.0), followed by washing twice with deionized water. Subsequently, 200 μL of EpCAM aptamer (1 μM) dispersed in TE buffer (pH 7.4) was added and incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h, followed by washing three times with deionized water to remove the unbound aptamers.

A 10 μL of MCF-7 derived exosomes at different concentrations (2 × 103-4 × 104 particles/μL) was added to the above EpCAM aptamer immobilized 96-well plate. And then the as-prepared TMB-GQDzymes@DFs were added and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h to form the sandwich structure of EpCAM aptamer/exosomes/TMB-GQDzymes@DFs, followed by washing three times with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to remove the unbound probes. Subsequently, 5 μL of H2O2 (5 mM) was added to each well and reacted for 40 min.

For colorimetric detection of exosomes, the UV-Vis absorption spectra of each well were measured, and the absorbance at 650 nm was recorded. The linear relationship was achieved by plotting exosome concentration versus the absorbance change (ΔA = A - A0, where A represents the absorbance in the presence of exosomes and A0 represents the absorbance in the absence of exosomes). For photothermal detection of exosomes, the sample in each plate was irradiated by 808 nm laser for 30 s in dark, which was immediately photographed with infrared thermography and the temperature was also recorded. The linear relationship was obtained by plotting exosome concentration versus the temperature change (ΔT = T - T0, where T represents the temperature in the presence of exosomes and T0 represents the temperature in the absence of exosomes).

The serum samples from breast cancer patients and healthy individuals were collected from Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University. Detection of exosomes in serum samples was carried out according to the same steps as described above. In brief, the serum was first diluted 100 times for further use. And then, 10 μL of the diluted serum was added to the EpCAM aptamer-immobilized 96-well plate, followed by adding TMB-GQDzymes@DFs. After introducing 5 μL of H2O2 (5 mM) to each well and reacted for 40 min, the absorbance change at 650 nm and the temperature change under laser irradiation of each sample were measured, respectively.

The GQDzymes with peroxidase-mimicking activity are synthesized through acid oxidation cutting of GO, and TMB is further immobilized on GQDzymes (TMB-GQDzymes) via π-π conjugation and electrostatic adsorption and the unbonded TMB is removed by ultra-filtration. The schematic diagram of the synthesis of TMB-GQDzymes is shown in Fig. 1 A. To investigate the morphology and size changes of GQDzymes before and after TMB immobilization, the TEM and AFM analysis were carried out. The GQDzymes are small monodispersed fragments with an average diameter of 50 nm in lateral size from TEM images and an average height of approximately 1.06 nm from AFM analysis (Fig. 1B and C). Compared with GQDzymes, the lateral size and shape of TMB-GQDzymes have hardly changed, whereas the average height of TMB-GQDzymes (2.28 nm) is higher than that of GQDzymes, indicating the conjugation of TMB on GQDzymes.

To prove the feasibility of TMB-GQDzymes for colorimetric assays, the absorbance changes of TMB, GQDzymes and TMB-GQDzymes at 650 nm were monitored upon addition of H2O2, respectively (Fig. 1D). The absorbance of TMB and GQDzymes at 650 nm show no significant change in 120 min, which increases rapidly for TMB-GQDzymes and reaches a plateau in ∼40 min. Thus, the peroxidase-like GQDzymes can catalyze H2O2 to generate ·OH, which then oxidizes the immobilized TMB to produce the absorbance change. In this study, the immobilization of TMB on GQDzymes can not only improve the dispersibility of TMB in water, but also boost the interaction between TMB and GQDzymes. It is worth noting that the absorbance of TMB-GQDzymes gradually increase with the increase amount of TMB (0.048–0.24 mg/mL) (Fig. 1E). When the concentration of TMB is at 0.144 mg/mL, the absorbance reaches the maximum. With further increase of the TMB concentration, the absorbance demonstrates little change, indicating that the immobilized TMB has reached the saturation when the added amount is at 0.144 mg/mL. The fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1F) confirms the presence of hydroxyl, carboxylic and epoxy groups on the surface of GQDzymes. What's more, the CO has a shift in TMB-GQDzymes (1720 cm−1) compared with GQDzymes (1665 cm−1), further implying the π-π conjugation between GQDzymes and TMB.

The as-prepared TMB-GQDzymes are further incorporated into DFs via RCA reaction to one-pot synthesize the DFs-encapsulated TMB-GQDzymes (TMB-GQDzymes@DFs) (Fig. 2 A). In brief, the two ends of template strand that contains the complementary sequence of CD63 aptamer are complementary to the primer strand. The template and primer are hybridized by annealing process and further circularized by T4 DNA ligase. With the assistance of phi29 DNA polymerase and dNTPs, the circular DNA template is amplified by RCA to obtain the long ssDNA and then self-assembled to form TMB-GQDzymes@DFs with the addition of TMB-GQDzymes. The resulting RCA products contain multiple CD63 aptamers for exosome capture. The non-denaturing polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was conducted to verify the RCA process and the formation of TMB-GQDzymes@DFs (Fig. 2B). Compared with the template strand (lane 1) and the primer strand (lane 2), the annealed template/primer hybrid shows a decreased electrophoretic mobility (lane 3). After the template is ligated by T4 DNA ligase, a clear band appears (lane 4), indicating that the template is successfully circularized to generate the circular template. In contrast, the TMB-GQDzymes@DFs with a large molecular weight cannot enter the gel by electrophoresis (lane 5), suggesting the successful preparation of DFs.

The morphologies and size of DFs before and after encapsulation of TMB-GQDzymes were investigated by SEM (Fig. 2C) and TEM (Fig. 2D), respectively. The DFs have good dispersibility and petal-like structure with an average size of 1.78 μm, which thus can effectively increase the specific surface area for highly efficient encapsulation of TMB-GQDzymes. Compared with DFs, the TMB-GQDzymes@DFs also exhibit a flower-like structure with a similar size as DFs, indicating that the encapsulation of TMB-GQDzymes has relatively little effect on the size of DFs. The zeta potential was further carried out to demonstrate the successful encapsulation of TMB-GQDzymes in DFs (Fig. 2E). The GQDzymes possess a negative charge of −51.2 mV, which are readily combined with positively charged TMB (0.4 mV) via electrostatic interaction. Compared to GQDzymes, the zeta potential of TMB-GQDzymes increases to −50.5 mV, indicating the combination of GQDzyme and TMB. In addition, the zeta potential value of TMB-GQDzymes@DFs (−44.0 mV) is significantly lower than that of DFs (−34.1 mV), indicating the successful encapsulation of TMB-GQDzymes into DFs. To further investigate the structure and composition of DFs and TMB-GQDzymes@DFs, scanning transmission electron microscope equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDS) was performed (Fig. 2F). The STEM-EDS elemental mapping results reveal the co-existence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), magnesium (Mg) and phosphorus (P) in both DFs and TMB-GQDzymes@DFs, which are mainly originated from DNA and Mg2PPi formed during the RCA process. The organic elemental analysis shows that the carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) contents are both increased in TMB-GQDzymes@DFs compared with DFs. Since TMB-GQDzymes are rich in C and N elements, this result indicates the successful encapsulation of TMB-GQDzymes in DFs (Fig. 2G).

In this study, the sandwich structure of EpCAM aptamer/exosomes/TMB-GQDzymes@DFs is constructed based on dual-aptamer recognition strategy, achieving sensitive and selective detection of exosomes. Firstly, the amino-modified EpCAM aptamers, which can specifically recognize and bind with the overexpressed EpCAM protein on the surface of MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes, are covalently immobilized on the bottom of each well of a 96-well plate via glutaraldehyde-mediated coupling. The amount of immobilized EpCAM aptamers is calculated as 2 × 10−11 mol/well (Fig. S2). Then, the TMB-GQDzymes@DFs containing multiple CD63 aptamers can also selectively capture the exosomes, which also play an important role as signal-generating tags. The catalytic principle of TMB-GQDzymes@DFs is shown in Fig. 3 A. In the presence of H2O2, the GQDzymes can oxidze TMB to produce oxTMB, which not only shows an obvious absorption peak at 650 nm, but also exhibits a near-infrared laser-driven photothermal effect. Therefore, a colorimetric/photothermal dual-mode biosensor is constructed for exosomes detection.

As the concentrations of exosomes increase from 0 to 4 × 104 particles/μL, an enhanced absorbance at 650 nm is observed with an obvious color change due to the increase of exosome-bound TMB-GQDzymes@DFs (Fig. 3B). There is a good linear relationship between ΔA (ΔA = A - A0, where A represents the absorbance in the presence of exosomes and A0 represents the absorbance in the absence of exosomes) and the concentration of exosomes (CExosomes) (Fig. 3C). The limit of detection (LOD) for the colorimetric assay is calculated as 1027 particles/μL (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of blank samples and s is the slope of working curve). Furthermore, the generated oxTMB shows the near-infrared laser-driven photothermal effect. With the increase of exosome concentration, the temperature under 808 nm laser irradiation is enhanced, which can be recorded by thermal imaging (Fig. 3D). A good linear relationship between the temperature increment (ΔT) (ΔT = T - T0, where T represents the temperature in the presence of exosomes and T0 represents the temperature in the absence of exosomes) and the concentration of exosomes (CExosomes) ranging from 2 × 103 to 4 × 104 particles/μL is obtained (Fig. 3E). Compared with the reported biosensors for exosome detection (Table S2), this dual-mode biosensor based on dual-aptamer recognition shows a high sensitivity with a wide detection range, which has great potential in real sample analysis.

Afterwards, the specificity for exosome detection was studied by measuring a series of common interfering substances using colorimetric mode and photothermal mode, respectively, including bovine serum albumin (BSA), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), glutathione (GSH), glucose, Na+ and K+. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, no apparent absorbance and temperature changes are observed for the interfering biomolecules, while a dramatic increase in absorbance and temperature is detected for MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes, demonstrating the satisfactory anti-interference ability of the constructed dual-mode biosensing platform. Additionally, the exosomes secreted from MCF-7 cells and L02 cells were also detected using this dual-mode biosensing platform, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the obvious absorbance and temperature enhancement are observed in the presence of MCF-7 secreted exosomes (2.5 × 104 particles/μL), while only little change in absorbance and temperature is detected upon the addition of exosomes secreted from L02 cells under the same concentration. These results reveal the excellent specificity of this dual-mode biosensing platform towards MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes based on dual-aptamer recognition, indicating the potential feasibility in cancer identification.

To assess the feasibility of the dual-readout biosensor for clinical sample analysis, the exosomes in human serum samples of breast cancer patients (n = 10) and healthy volunteers (n = 10) were analyzed directly. In this assay, the standard exosomes were replaced by 100-fold diluted human serum samples, and the absorbance at 650 nm and temperature under 808 nm laser irradiation were recorded (Fig. 5 A and B). As expected, both the absorbance at 650 nm and temperature of the serums from breast cancer patients have demonstrated a significant increase, while a negligible enhancement is observed in those groups from healthy volunteers (Fig. 5C and D). This suggests that the content of MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes in the serum of breast cancer patients is higher, which has significant difference compared with those from healthy volunteers. Overall, the proposed colorimetric and photothermal dual-mode exosomes detection method can distinguish breast cancer patients from healthy controls with high accuracy, which has great potential for future clinical applications in cancer diagnosis.

To sum up, based on the DFs-encapsulated nanozyme probe and dual-aptamer recognition strategy, a colorimetric and photothermal dual-mode biosensor has been constructed for sensitive and selective detection of MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes with many remarkable advantages. Firstly, the DFs encapsulated-nanozymes achieve the dual-function of recognition elements and signal-generating tags. On the one hand, the circular DNA containing the complementary sequence of CD63 aptamer is utilized as a template to produce repeated sequences of CD63 aptamer through the RCA self-assembly reaction, which can improve the capture efficiency of exosomes significantly; on the other hand, TMB is immobilized on the surface of GQDzymes, which can not only increase the water solubility of TMB, but also shorten the distance between TMB and nanozymes to facilitate the catalytic reaction. Secondly, the EpCAM aptamers immobilized on 96-well plate can specifically recognize and capture MCF-7 cell-derived exosomes, which further improve the detection selectivity. Finally, the proposed exosome sensing platform is readily applied for identification of breast cancer patients from healthy groups by analyzing the real serum samples, showing high sensitivity and accuracy. Overall, this method can be expanded to disease-related exosome detection by replacing the sequences of recognition aptamers, which provides a simple and universal platform for disease diagnosis in practical applications.

Xiaoyue Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation; Xueying Zhu: Methodology, Investigation, Yuanfang Li: Investigation, Validation, Xin Hai: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Sai Bi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004077 and 22076087), the Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (ZR2020JQ08), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671988), the Youth Innovation Technology Program of Shandong Province (2019KJC029), and the Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE (M2022-6).
